skill.name: langgraph
skill.description: Expert in LangGraph - the production-grade framework for building stateful, multi-actor AI applications. Covers graph construction, state management, cycles and branches, persistence with checkpointing, human-in-the-loop patterns, and tool integration.
skill.summary: Expert in LangGraph for building production-grade AI agents with visible, debuggable graph structures and state management.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The skill is a legitimate LangGraph documentation and assistance skill. One true positive was found: an eval() call in example code (line 67) that accepts user input without sanitization. This is documented example code showing tool implementation, but poses a risk if users copy this pattern. Multiple false positives were dismissed: Ruby/shell backtick execution (markdown code fences misinterpreted) and weak cryptographic algorithms (keyword matches on 'hash' in framework terminology).

high_findings.title: Dynamic Code Execution with eval()
high_findings.description: The calculator tool example uses eval(expression) on line 67, which accepts user input without sanitization. This is a security risk if users copy this pattern for production code. However, this is example/demonstration code in documentation, not malicious runtime code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct eval() call on user-provided expression parameter without input validation. This is a genuine security vulnerability pattern, though it's in example code meant to demonstrate tool creation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at multiple lines. These are markdown code fences (```python) not actual Ruby code. The backticks are markdown syntax for code blocks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown code fences use triple backticks for syntax highlighting. No Ruby or shell execution exists in this Python skill file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' at multiple lines. These are false positives - the scanner matched on 'hash' keywords in 'checkpoint' and 'TypedDict' (Python typing) terminology, not actual cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Scanner matched on 'hash' in 'checkpointing' and framework terminology. No cryptographic algorithms or weak crypto exist in this skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: eval() on User Input
dangerous_patterns.description: Calculator tool example uses eval() on expression parameter without sanitization

content.value_statement: LangGraph is a production-grade framework for building stateful, multi-actor AI applications. This skill helps you design agent architectures with visible graph structures, manage complex state, implement cycles and branches, and add persistence for resumable conversations.
actual_capabilities: Create LangGraph agent architectures using StateGraph with nodes and edges
Implement state management with TypedDict and custom reducers for accumulation
Build conditional routing logic to direct agent flow based on state
Add checkpointing and persistence for resumable conversations
Integrate tools and tool calling into agent workflows
Implement human-in-the-loop patterns for agent oversight
limitations: Python-only (TypeScript SDK is in early development)
Requires understanding of graph concepts and state management
Does not provide LLM access - requires separate API keys for OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.
Debugging complex graphs can be challenging in production

use_cases.title: Build Multi-Step Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Create agents that can reason, use tools, and maintain conversation context across multiple interactions.
use_cases.target_user: AI developers building production AI agents

use_cases.title: Implement Research Agent Systems
use_cases.description: Design agents that can gather information from multiple sources, merge findings, and produce synthesized reports.
use_cases.target_user: Developers creating research and analysis automation

use_cases.title: Create Stateful Chat Applications
use_cases.description: Build chat applications that remember conversation history and can resume from previous states.
use_cases.target_user: Engineers building persistent conversational AI apps

prompt_templates.title: Basic Agent Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple ReAct-style agent using LangGraph that can call tools. Show me how to define state, create nodes, set up edges, and compile the graph.

prompt_templates.title: State Management
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to manage complex state in LangGraph using TypedDict and custom reducers. I want to accumulate messages, merge findings from multiple nodes, and track conversation history.

prompt_templates.title: Conditional Routing
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me implement conditional branching in LangGraph. I need to route queries to different agents based on their type (coding, search, chat) and handle the responses appropriately.

prompt_templates.title: Persistence Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Add checkpointing to my LangGraph agent so conversations can be resumed. Show me how to configure a checkpointer and persist state between sessions.

output_examples.input: Create a simple agent with tools
output_examples.output:
Here's a basic LangGraph agent setup:

1. Define AgentState with TypedDict
2. Create tool functions with @tool decorator
3. Build agent node that calls LLM with tools
4. Add conditional edges for tool execution
5. Compile and run the graph

The agent will loop between the LLM and tools until no more tools are called, then return to the user.

output_examples.input: How do I prevent infinite loops?
output_examples.output:
Use a max iterations counter in your state:

1. Add 'iterations' field to your state
2. Create should_continue function that checks iterations
3. Increment counter in your agent node
4. Return END when iterations exceed limit

This ensures your agent has a clear exit condition and won't run forever.

best_practices: Always define clear exit conditions in your routing functions to prevent infinite loops
Use reducers appropriately - add_messages for accumulation, custom functions for complex merging
Design input/output schemas to keep state minimal and focused on what's needed
anti_patterns: Using eval() on user input in tools - always validate and sanitize any user-provided expressions
Creating stateless nodes that don't return state updates - defeats the purpose of LangGraph
Using giant monolithic state with unnecessary fields - causes serialization overhead and context bloat

faq.question: What is LangGraph?
faq.answer: LangGraph is a framework for building stateful, multi-actor AI applications using graph structures. It extends LangChain with support for cycles and persistence.

faq.question: Do I need an LLM API key?
faq.answer: Yes, LangGraph requires access to an LLM API (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) for the agent to function. The skill provides the architecture; you provide the API credentials.

faq.question: Can LangGraph save conversation history?
faq.answer: Yes, LangGraph supports checkpointing with various backends (Memory, SQLite, Postgres). This allows agents to resume from previous states.

faq.question: Is LangGraph Python-only?
faq.answer: The primary SDK is Python. A TypeScript/JavaScript SDK exists but is in early development stages.

faq.question: How do I add tools to my agent?
faq.answer: Define tools using the @tool decorator from langchain_core.tools. Bind them to your LLM with bind_tools(), then create a ToolNode to handle execution.

faq.question: What's the difference between edges and conditional edges?
faq.answer: Regular edges connect nodes in a fixed path. Conditional edges use a function to determine which node to go to next based on the current state.
